event: Early Neolithic farming communities such as Tell Abu Hureyra develop some of the world's first settled agriculture along the Euphrates.
Tell Halaf culture flourishes in northern Syria, producing distinctive painted pottery across the region.
The city of Ebla rises as a major commercial and political power, leaving a vast cuneiform archive.
Sargon of Akkad's empire extends control over Syrian city-states including Ebla and Mari.
The kingdom of Yamhad, centered on Aleppo, dominates northern Syria during the Middle Bronze Age.
The coastal city of Ugarit thrives and develops one of the earliest known alphabets.
The Battle of Kadesh is fought near the Orontes between Egypt under Ramesses II and the Hittite Empire.
Bronze Age cities including Ugarit collapse amid the upheavals of the Sea Peoples and regional crisis.
Aramaean kingdoms such as Aram-Damascus emerge, spreading the Aramaic language across the Near East.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire conquers Damascus, ending the independent kingdom of Aram-Damascus.
Syria passes to the Neo-Babylonian Empire following the fall of Assyria.
The Achaemenid Persian Empire incorporates Syria after Cyrus the Great's conquests.
Alexander the Great defeats Persia at Issus, bringing Syria under Macedonian control.
Seleucus I founds the Seleucid Empire, making Syria its heartland and later building Antioch as capital.
The Roman general Pompey annexes Syria, establishing it as a Roman province.
Rome annexes the Nabataean kingdom, and the desert city of Palmyra grows into a wealthy caravan hub.
Emperor Aurelian crushes the rebellion of Queen Zenobia and sacks Palmyra.
Syria becomes part of the Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire after the division of Rome.
Arab Muslim forces defeat the Byzantines at the Battle of Yarmuk, beginning Islamic rule over Syria.
The Umayyad Caliphate is established with Damascus as the capital of the Islamic empire.
The Abbasid revolution overthrows the Umayyads and shifts the caliphate's capital to Iraq.
The Fatimid Caliphate extends its influence over parts of Syria.
Crusaders capture Antioch and establish Crusader states along the Syrian coast.
Saladin takes control of Damascus, uniting Syria and Egypt under Ayyubid rule.
The Mamluks defeat the Mongols at Ain Jalut and assert control over Syria.
Timur (Tamerlane) sacks Aleppo and Damascus, devastating the region.
The Ottomans defeat the Mamluks at Marj Dabiq, bringing Syria under Ottoman rule for four centuries.
Egyptian forces under Muhammad Ali's son Ibrahim Pasha occupy Syria until 1840.
Sectarian violence in Damascus and Mount Lebanon prompts European intervention.
The secret Sykes-Picot Agreement divides Ottoman Arab lands into French and British spheres of influence.
Arab and Allied forces capture Damascus, ending Ottoman rule during World War I.
France defeats Faisal's short-lived Arab Kingdom of Syria and the League of Nations grants France a mandate.
The Great Syrian Revolt erupts against French colonial rule, lasting until 1927.
A Franco-Syrian treaty promising independence is negotiated but never ratified by France.
Syria becomes a founding member of the United Nations and the Arab League.
The last French troops withdraw and Syria becomes fully independent on 17 April.
A series of military coups begins with Husni al-Zaim, inaugurating decades of political instability.
Syria merges with Egypt to form the United Arab Republic under Gamal Abdel Nasser.
A coup dissolves the union with Egypt and restores Syrian independence.
The Ba'ath Party seizes power in a coup, beginning its long dominance of Syrian politics.
Israel captures the Golan Heights from Syria during the Six-Day War.
Hafez al-Assad takes power in the 'Corrective Movement', founding a decades-long family rule.
Syria launches the October War against Israel in an attempt to retake the Golan Heights.
Syrian troops intervene in the Lebanese Civil War, beginning a long military presence in Lebanon.
The Syrian government crushes a Muslim Brotherhood uprising in Hama with massive loss of life.
Hafez al-Assad dies and his son Bashar al-Assad succeeds him as president.
Syria withdraws its forces from Lebanon after the assassination of Rafik Hariri and mass protests.
Pro-democracy protests during the Arab Spring escalate into a devastating civil war.
The Islamic State seizes large swaths of Syrian territory and declares a caliphate from Raqqa.
Russia intervenes militarily on behalf of the Assad government, shifting the war's momentum.
Government forces backed by Russia and Iran recapture eastern Aleppo after a prolonged siege.
A rebel offensive rapidly captures Damascus, toppling Bashar al-Assad and ending over fifty years of Assad family rule.
